question: Q9
title: Land-Use Buffer Characteristics and Environmental Associations
research_question: Look at the landuse buffer characteristics across sites and whether they are associated with PM2.5 and heat indicators.

pm25_associations.interpretation: Road proximity explains 46.2% of inter-site PM2.5 variance

trees_50m.note: Counterintuitive positive — likely confounded with inland/sheltered locations

impervious_25m.note: Counterintuitive negative — heavily paved sites near waterfront benefit from maritime cooling

limitations: Small sample size (n=12 sites) limits statistical power
Small buffer zones (25-50m) may miss broader urban context
Urban homogeneity limits variance in land-use variables
Waterfront proximity and wind corridors confound WBGT associations
Temporal aggregation collapses rich variation into site-level means
key_findings: Road proximity is dominant PM2.5 driver: 50m buffer r=0.680 (p=0.015), R2=46.2%
No land-use variable significantly predicts WBGT — microscale factors dominate
Counterintuitive WBGT associations likely reflect waterfront proximity confounding
Two land-use clusters identified but no significant environmental outcome differences
Environmental justice: traffic management and buffer vegetation near high-road sites could reduce PM2.5 exposure
